failure_surface: Agent repeatedly fails with SyntaxError when attempting to execute Python code for spreadsheet manipulation. Every tool invocation produces an invalid syntax error at line 1, preventing any data loading or processing.
root_cause: Agent incorrectly uses the web_search tool for executing Python code. The web_search tool expects search query text, not executable Python statements. Code intended for spreadsheet manipulation was passed as a search query, causing the execution environment to treat it as invalid syntax rather than runnable code.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Select the appropriate code execution tool (e.g., execute_python, run_code) when intending to run Python scripts for spreadsheet operations, rather than using discovery/search tools designed for web queries.
evidence: Multiple consecutive attempts show identical SyntaxError pattern: 'Cell In[1], line 1' followed by '^ SyntaxError: invalid syntax'
Tool name 'web_search' is used consistently across all failed attempts while passing Python code in the query parameter
The traceback points to line 1 beginning with '<function=web_search>' suggesting the tool cannot interpret the embedded Python import statements
No successful tool execution occurred before task termination - zero progress on actual spreadsheet manipulation

memory_items.title: Tool Selection for Code Execution
memory_items.lesson: When performing spreadsheet manipulation requiring Python code, use the dedicated code execution tool, not search or web browsing tools.
memory_items.generalization: Search and code-execution tools serve distinct purposes - selecting the wrong category prevents any downstream task execution regardless of code correctness.

memory_items.title: Early Validation Tool Output
memory_items.lesson: Verify tool output after first execution attempt; repeated identical errors indicate wrong tool choice, not fixable by retrying same code.
memory_items.generalization: Persistent SyntaxError or execution failures on tool invocation suggest fundamental tool selection error before debugging code logic.

memory_items.title: Code Formatting for Executable Tools
memory_items.lesson: Executable tools expect pure code input; avoid embedding natural language explanations or comments within the code parameter sent to execution tools.
memory_items.generalization: Mixed content (comments, explanations, code) in execution parameters can cause parsing failures that prevent script evaluation.

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### Tool Selection Principles
- **Distinguish execution from discovery**: Code-execution tools accept Python statements; search/discovery tools accept natural-language queries. Using a search tool for code execution results in immediate syntax failures.
- **Validate first attempt**: When a tool returns consistent errors across multiple tries, verify the tool capability matches the task type before rewriting code.
- **Keep execution parameters clean**: Execute-only tools expect pure code input without explanatory text mixed into the code block.
